sentiment.rationale: A devastating locust plague strips the land bare; the prophet calls priests and people to weep, fast, and mourn before the Lord.
The day of the Lord comes as an invading army, but God promises restoration if the people rend their hearts; culminates in the outpouring of the Spirit.
God judges the nations in the valley of decision, vindicates His people, and establishes Zion as a place of refuge and abundance.

summaries.summary: A catastrophic locust plague devastates the land; Joel calls for national mourning, fasting, and repentance as the day of the Lord approaches.
The day of the Lord is described as a great and terrible army; the people are called to rend their hearts, and God promises to pour out His Spirit on all flesh.
God gathers the nations to the valley of Jehoshaphat for judgment, delivers His people, and establishes Zion as a place of eternal refuge.

crossReferences.note: Peter quotes this prophecy at Pentecost, declaring the outpouring of the Spirit as fulfillment.
Paul quotes Joel's promise that whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be delivered.
The harvest and winepress imagery parallels the final judgment in Revelation.

verses.reason: A call for a solemn assembly with fasting and crying unto the Lord.
'Rend your heart, and not your garments'—a call for inward repentance.
'I will pour out my spirit upon all flesh'—the foundational prophecy of Pentecost and latter-day gifts.
'Multitudes, multitudes in the valley of decision'—the great final judgment of nations.
